metadata.title: Measuring and mitigating systemic risks: how the forging of new alliances between central bank and academic economists legitimize the transnational macroprudential agenda

abstract: After the great financial crisis of 2007-2009, central banks were handed a macroprudential mandate to contain systemic risks, a mandate seen as endangering their independence due to expected distributional conflicts. At the same time, depoliticization through scientific expertise was largely foreclosed, as systemic risk was a largely undefined concept. This paper focuses on how central banks dealt with this conundrum. It examines the scientific debate on systemic risk and macroprudential regulation post-crisis, focusing on the debate's impact on final regulation. Employing author-topic-modeling on a unique dataset of 2397 published economic papers on the relevant topics, we detect the formation of a new alliance between central bankers and academic economists working jointly on developing systemic risk measures. Centered around a hinge of systemic risk contribution by individual banks, this new alliance expresses itself by incorporating the macroprudential concerns of practitioners into abstract market-based systemic risk measures. These measures develop incrementally, using and repurposing techniques from financial economics pre-crisis to legitimize and justify macroprudential interventions post-crisis. This alliance allows us to account for the incremental change witnessed post-crisis and point to its potential for long-term fundamental change.

paragraphs: Prior to the great financial crisis of 2007-2009, central banks had achieved an unknown degree of autonomy and depoliticization (King, 2005;Marcussen, 2009;Watson, 2002). Couching their action in the language of economics as a science, coupled with a 'self-regulatory' consensus that increasingly based banking regulation on banks' private risk management tools such as Value at Risk (Lockwood, 2015), central banks presided over a golden age of central banking called the 'Great Moderation' (Bernanke, 2004). The financial crisis put an end to both this tranquility and the self-regulatory consensus. Economics as a science had ignored the potential for the build-up of financial instability during tranquil times and 'market self-regulation', to the surprise of its proponents, had proved a failure (Greenspan, 2008). The costs of the widespread central banking stance of 'post-hoc interventionism' regarding the build-up of financial risks (Golub et al., 2015) proved punishing, in turn, undermining the expert authority of economics and central banks. And yet, in a paradoxical development, central banks saw their mandates extended, not cut back, when they were tasked by the G20 in 2009 to design and implement a framework for dealing with systemic risks going forward (Financial Stability Board, 2009;Lombardi & Moschella, 2017).

Central banks not only had the task of dealing with the immediate effects of the crises, but also of developing new frameworks to deal with financial instability and systemic risk going forward, adding to their being alone in the face of unexpected financial instability (Mabbett & Schelkle, 2019). This new role, which implied both distributional conflicts with homeowners as well as banks (Goodhart, 2015), threatened to be a poisoned chalice, repoliticizing central banking (Baker, 2018;Marcussen, 2013). Acting on this mandate required central banks to agree on a common conception of systemic risk and how to measure it, a task made more complicated by the fact that these notions were remarkably undefined in mainstream academic discourse (Helleiner, 2014;Hellwig, 2014;Thiemann, Aldegwy, et al., 2018). Concerned about the reactivity of financial markets and the possibility of unintended consequences (Stellinga, 2020;Stellinga & M€ ugge, 2017) as well as the fear of political backlash (Tucker, 2018), central bankers were only too aware that their macroprudential intervention would require careful legitimation based, if possible, on a scientific framework for measuring and mitigating systemic risks (Goodhart, 2015, p. 287;Marcussen, 2013, p. 29). Yet, when looking to financial economics, the scientific proposals on offer looked dim (M€ ugge, 2013).

While the concept of macroprudential regulation had been present in the discourse of central banks and international organizations such as the Bank of International Settlements (BIS) since the early 2000s (e.g. Borio, 2003;Crockett, 2000), they were not formatted in mathematical models or language and therefore did not garner much attention in mainstream policymaking or academia (Thiemann, Aldegwy, et al., 2018). Furthermore, this new approach contradicted the microprudential focus of prior models of financial regulation, which maintained that if all individual institutions were sound, the overall system would be sound. As identification and measurement of systemic risk were intended to provide the basis for the use of macroprudential tools to limit the build-up of risk (Baker, 2013a;IMF-FSB-BIS, 2016), central banks faced a conundrum. Having had their reputation based on technical rationality (Abolafia, 2012) and economic expertisewhich they had also built in-house (Marcussen, 2009) they were to act on a largely undeveloped field in terms of economics (Tarullo, 2013;Thiemann, 2019): 'squishy science', in the words of a Fed official (as cited in Goodhart, 2015, p. 295). Having little to fall back on in regards to mainstream economic models, they were exposing themselves to charges of arbitrariness and lack of expertise.

Given this conundrum, this paper traces the professional origins of different systemic risk measures and models that were developed after the crisis, especially those that have come to justify regulatory measures after the crisis. We ask which groups of economists proposed what kind of measurement of systemic risk, and what characterizes the measurement of systemic risk that finally informed policymaking. If central banks were to establish a macroprudential framework that took seriously broader conceptualizations of systemic risks, they would have to fundamentally depart from pre-crisis regulatory and economic mainstream assumptions (Baker, 2013a) and develop a new framework to measure and mitigate these risks. And yet, to shield policymakers from legitimacy threats, the literature maintains that it is centrally important that these new measures and models are deemed 'objective' and based on quasi-scientific evidence (Jasanoff, 2012;Porter, 1995). Achieving objectivity for such a regulatory framework, however, could hardly be the work of central bank economists alone, given the perceived conflict of interest of in-house science justifying regulation (Tucker, 2018) and the epistemic inferiority of central bank economists to academic economists (Fourcade, 2009;Whitley, 2000), requiring intensive boundary work between them on this question (Adrian, 2018).

We draw on a unique dataset of 2397 articles on systemic risk and macroprudential regulation compiled from the RePEc database to investigate how this boundary work unfolded and to what degree the systemic risk measures drawn upon by central banks were molded by internal central bank-driven research, by academia, or by cooperation between the two. We use author-topic modeling and document analysis to identify central themes in the economic discourse on systemic risk measurements. We complement quantitative text and citation analysis with careful readings of the document corpus and nine interviews with prominent economists and regulators working on these matters. Our research thereby speaks to the performative power of economics in shaping regulation (Hirschman & Popp-Berman, 2014), the shifting alliance of academic economists and central banks post-crisis, and the dynamics of the macroprudential policy reforms post-crisis (Baker, 2018), particularly regarding the underlying economic input. We find a new alliance between central bank economists and academics, who seek to justify macroprudential interventions combining pre-crisis methods with the new macroprudential ideas about the financial system. This explains both the incremental change post-crisis as well as its potential for long-term changes.

In this paper, we first detail how economic ideas informed policymaking pre-and post-crisis and present our theoretical framework vis-a-vis the intellectual field of economics. We then introduce our dataset and method, with the following section presenting the results, distinguishing different approaches to systemic risk measurement based on their content and the authors' professional affiliation. We subsequently compare the most prominent systemic risk measures regarding their alignment with macroprudential concerns over the use of market data, detailing the nature of the new alliance of central bankers and academics and its partially paradoxical content. We conclude by placing this new epistemic alliance that undergirds the macroprudential paradigm in the context of the literature on post-crisis macroprudential regulation, pointing to its potential for long-run fundamental change.

sections.header_raw: The role of economic ideas within pre-and post-crisis financial regulation
sections.header_norm: the role of economic ideas within pre and post crisis financial regulation
paragraphs: How do economic ideas feed into financial regulation? Policymakers increasingly elaborate financial regulation based on economic knowledge and ideas offered to them by the field of economics (Black, 2013;Harnay & Scialom, 2016;Jasanoff, 2011b). They do so because they depend on 'scientific, evidence-based' knowledge to justify their regulatory interventions (Jasanoff, 2011b). In this way, economic ideas have a legitimizing role, protecting policymakers against charges of arbitrariness or bias (Boehmer-Christiansen, 1995;Jasanoff, 2011a;Hirschman & Popp-Berman, 2014). Economics in this respect provides an ideational infrastructure that defines the role and scope of legitimate public intervention in the economy to achieve certain goals, which in turn is usually derived from the array of contemporary mainstream economic theories (Ban, 2015;Braun, 2014, p. 51;Clift, 2018Clift, , 2019)). But economic knowledge does not feed into policymaking in a linear way, nor are economic ideas similar in content and authority (Ban, 2015;Kentikelenis & Seabrooke, 2017). Instead, they must be filtered and translated by experts (Bradshaw & Borchers, 2000;Chwieroth, 2010;Jasanoff, 2011b;Spruijt et al., 2014). This gives experts considerable discretion in selecting which ideas flow into the policy sphere and how they are translated into policy-suitable knowledge (Ban, 2015;Clift, 2018).

Experts select ideas based on the fit with perceived local needs and capabilities of the implementing agency (Jasanoff, 2011b, Porter, 1995). Decisive criteria can be data availability (Kranke & Yarrow, 2019), but also the personal interest in career advancement of professionals dealing with the issue of financial stability (Seabrooke & Tsingou, 2020). Experts selecting ideas for regulation can form alliances with surrounding professions dealing with the issue of financial stability in private finance and/or academia (Seabrooke & Tsingou, 2020), which, if sufficiently powerful, 'can create the dominant understanding on how an issue should best and most legitimately be treated' (Seabrooke & Tsingou, 2020, p. 5). These alliances are often based on 'hinges': shared conceptions whose promotion produces rewards in allied fields, thereby enabling professionals to advance their careers (Abbott, 2005;Seabrooke & Tsingou, 2015). A hinge between policymakers and financial industry professionals emerged before the crisis, treating the achievement of financial stability as 'a matter of empowering regulation as risk management' by individual banks (Seabrooke & Tsingou, 2020, p. 16). It rewarded policymakers by allowing them to project expertise in the context of rapid financial innovation and rewarded financial market professionals with the advantages of self-monitoring.

This hinge emerged because of the political-economic situation constraining the capacity of regulators to intervene in financial markets at that time and the allure of recently developed private risk management techniques created in the 1990s. Pre-crisis, regulatory agencies had difficulties keeping up with private financial innovations and their implications for system stability (Thiemann, 2018). Yet, at the same time, the agencies saw their capacity to limit these processes constrained by political will and the endorsement of innovation by high-level regulators such as Greenspan (Tsingou, 2004). Given the agencies' lack of data and understanding of the systemic consequences of innovation, they increasingly placed hopes for containing systemic risks on market discipline and market-based supervision (Tsingou, 2004), embracing both private risk transfer and private risk management techniques. Consequently, to compensate for their lack of technical expertise, policymakers integrated developments in private risk modeling (VaR models)jointly developed with academiainto the global Basel framework (Seabrooke & Tsingou, 2020). They did so first in the market risk amendment in 1996 and later for the treatment of credit risks in Basel II (Adrian, 2018;Lockwood, 2015). In this way, 'central bankers implicitly recognized that risk management models in use by major banks are far more advanced than anything they could ever propose (Jorion, 1997a, p. 41)' (Lockwood, 2015, p. 723).

Based on a simplified version of the Efficient Market Hypothesis (Turner, 2011), this pre-crisis regulatory approach excluded macroprudential problems of liquidity risks and pro-cyclical tendencies of the financial system that emerge from systemwide developments (Stellinga & M€ ugge, 2017, p. 17). Instead, the focus was on the microprudential risk management of individual institutions. Ensuring the safety and soundness of these institutions was mostly delegated to the large banks themselves, as private bankers had succeeded in using their private risk management systems to portray themselves as responsible and reliable risk managers (De Goede, 2004;Lockwood, 2015). As an authoritative claim to control risks and limit future losses, these models produced 'an easily understood number: the maximum possible loss on a portfolio likely to occur a given percent of the time' (Lockwood, 2015, p. 722), excluding tail risks from consideration. Due to its assumptions and its exclusive focus on short-term volatility which allowed banks to increase risktaking as volatility went down, this regulatory framework and the Value at Risk model it used to contain systemic risk widely underestimated the tail risks to the pre-crisis system (Lockwood, 2015), leaving regulators and the financial system unprepared for their realization.

The crisis dynamics then empowered critics in academia and among central bankers, who for several years had pointed to the dangers of an exclusively microprudential approach and the pro-cyclical tendencies of Value at Risk measures, with a radical ideational shift taking place in the way regulators and central bankers perceived the financial system (Baker, 2013a). And yet, this radical ideational shift was not accompanied by a concomitant operational consensus on how to implement such a macroprudential approach (Baker, 2013b). Instead, such a consensus was seen to evolve incrementally (Johnson et al., 2019) due to the need to convince microprudential regulators of the merits of the new approach and the need to establish scientific evidence underpinning regulatory interventions (Baker, 2013b(Baker, , 2018;;Moschella & Tsingou, 2013). In particular, the need to produce scientific evidence, on whose basis regulators could seek to measure and detect systemic risks, proved to be an encumbering factor (Thiemann, Birk, et al., 2018). In a bureaucratic environment characterized by scientization, these new ideas had to be operationalized, reconciliated with existing models, and corroborated by evidence (Clift, 2018, p. 39f) before they could serve as foundations for regulatory devices that allowed regulators to visualize systemic risk and justify measures to contain it (Hirschman & Popp-Berman, 2014).

The macroprudential policy shift can therefore best be characterized as a fragmented change, 'whereby a crisis may induce rapid change in one specific dimension of a paradigm … at some point, while another dimension develops slowly in response to cumulative gradual pressures' (Kaya & Reay, 2019, p. 392). Given the devastating consequences of VaR and its political-economic ramifications, this gradual change in regulatory devices poses the question of whose expertise flowed into these new regulatory devices to measure systemic risk. The answer, we argue, is at least partially determined by shifting alliances of epistemic groups in the field of economics and its underlying work incentives (see also Seabrooke & Tsingou, 2020).

They did so first in the market risk amendment in 1996 and later for the treatment of credit risks in Basel II (Adrian, 2018;Lockwood, 2015). In this way, 'central bankers implicitly recognized that risk management models in use by major banks are far more advanced than anything they could ever propose (Jorion, 1997a, p. 41)' (Lockwood, 2015, p. 723).

Instead, the focus was on the microprudential risk management of individual institutions. Ensuring the safety and soundness of these institutions was mostly delegated to the large banks themselves, as private bankers had succeeded in using their private risk management systems to portray themselves as responsible and reliable risk managers (De Goede, 2004;Lockwood, 2015). As an authoritative claim to control risks and limit future losses, these models produced 'an easily understood number: the maximum possible loss on a portfolio likely to occur a given percent of the time' (Lockwood, 2015, p. 722), excluding tail risks from consideration. Due to its assumptions and its exclusive focus on short-term volatility which allowed banks to increase risktaking as volatility went down, this regulatory framework and the Value at Risk model it used to contain systemic risk widely underestimated the tail risks to the pre-crisis system (Lockwood, 2015), leaving regulators and the financial system unprepared for their realization.

And yet, this radical ideational shift was not accompanied by a concomitant operational consensus on how to implement such a macroprudential approach (Baker, 2013b). Instead, such a consensus was seen to evolve incrementally (Johnson et al., 2019) due to the need to convince microprudential regulators of the merits of the new approach and the need to establish scientific evidence underpinning regulatory interventions (Baker, 2013b(Baker, , 2018;;Moschella & Tsingou, 2013). In particular, the need to produce scientific evidence, on whose basis regulators could seek to measure and detect systemic risks, proved to be an encumbering factor (Thiemann, Birk, et al., 2018). In a bureaucratic environment characterized by scientization, these new ideas had to be operationalized, reconciliated with existing models, and corroborated by evidence (Clift, 2018, p. 39f) before they could serve as foundations for regulatory devices that allowed regulators to visualize systemic risk and justify measures to contain it (Hirschman & Popp-Berman, 2014).

The macroprudential policy shift can therefore best be characterized as a fragmented change, 'whereby a crisis may induce rapid change in one specific dimension of a paradigm … at some point, while another dimension develops slowly in response to cumulative gradual pressures' (Kaya & Reay, 2019, p. 392). Given the devastating consequences of VaR and its political-economic ramifications, this gradual change in regulatory devices poses the question of whose expertise flowed into these new regulatory devices to measure systemic risk. The answer, we argue, is at least partially determined by shifting alliances of epistemic groups in the field of economics and its underlying work incentives (see also Seabrooke & Tsingou, 2020).

How do economic ideas feed into financial regulation? Policymakers increasingly elaborate financial regulation based on economic knowledge and ideas offered to them by the field of economics (Black, 2013;Harnay & Scialom, 2016;Jasanoff, 2011b). They do so because they depend on 'scientific, evidence-based' knowledge to justify their regulatory interventions (Jasanoff, 2011b). In this way, economic ideas have a legitimizing role, protecting policymakers against charges of arbitrariness or bias (Boehmer-Christiansen, 1995;Jasanoff, 2011a;Hirschman & Popp-Berman, 2014). Economics in this respect provides an ideational infrastructure that defines the role and scope of legitimate public intervention in the economy to achieve certain goals, which in turn is usually derived from the array of contemporary mainstream economic theories (Ban, 2015;Braun, 2014, p. 51;Clift, 2018Clift, , 2019)). But economic knowledge does not feed into policymaking in a linear way, nor are economic ideas similar in content and authority (Ban, 2015;Kentikelenis & Seabrooke, 2017). Instead, they must be filtered and translated by experts (Bradshaw & Borchers, 2000;Chwieroth, 2010;Jasanoff, 2011b;Spruijt et al., 2014). This gives experts considerable discretion in selecting which ideas flow into the policy sphere and how they are translated into policy-suitable knowledge (Ban, 2015;Clift, 2018).

sections.header_raw: Shifting alliances of academic and applied economists
sections.header_norm: shifting alliances of academic and applied economists
paragraphs: According to Whitley, economics divides into a 'scientific' field and a much larger 'intellectual' field (2000, see also Ban & Patenaude, 2019). The scientific field refers to research conducted within universities by academics who are subject to the incentive regime of academia (Ban et al., 2016). The purpose of conducting research is to engage in scientific discussion with peers and publish in prestigious journals, which often involve mathematical models based on equilibrium thinking (Breslau, 1997). Commonly, these models are mathematically complex and tend to strongly simplify real-world situations to keep models traceable (Morgan, 2012). The intellectual fieldbroader in scopealso comprises non-academic economic research within public and private organizations, such as central banks and international organizations. The latter are different in the way they conduct research, due to different work-related incentives and rewards for economists focusing on particular policy problems at hand which invites flexibility vis-a-vis overarching, dogmatic economic ideas 1 (Ban & Patenaude, 2019;Braun, 2016;Reay, 2012). Crucially, they also produce economic knowledge.

The research mandate for these applied economists is often to maximize realworld applicability, e.g. to derive useful policy prescriptions rather than produce a scientifically novel argument or insight. Applied economists are therefore much less constrained by academic markers of professional excellencelike applying the latest economic model or econometric techniqueand it is much easier for them to cross research boundaries and engage with non-formal kinds of reasoning, such as historical observations (Thiemann, Aldegwy, et al., 2018). However, non-formal reasoning lacks the epistemic authority to establish scientific boundary objects, which is why these economists rely at least partially on academics and their quantitative and modeling skills to generate epistemically authoritative statements employable within policymaking (Gieryn, 1983;Jasanoff, 1987;Merton, 1945). Precrisis, this epistemic inferiority of central bankers and regulators, who often lacked the technical expertise to build risk models themselves (Lockwood, 2015, p. 723;Tsingou, 2004), led to alliances with professional economists in academia and private finance (Ban et al., 2016;Seabrooke & Tsingou, 2014, 2020), paving the way for the inclusion of private risk models in financial regulation.

Post-crisis, it was the failure of these risk frameworkswidely seen in the expert community as contributing to the build-up of systemic risksthat validated early critics (Danielsson, 2000;Persaud, 2001). As predicted, these models grossly underestimated risks that were accumulating in the financial system and even procyclically reinforced them (Lockwood, 2015) due to the models' incapacity to envision the amplification of risks within the financial system (Adrian & Shin, 2010). Given this widespread criticism of private risk models and the mainstream vision of financial economics that assumed efficient, ever-liquid financial markets both in academia and by central bankers, the development of systemic risk models could hardly stem from private finance (cf. Adrian, 2018). Therefore, academia was de facto the only option left for policymakers to look for ideas, inspiration and legitimization in their quest to develop systemic risk models. However, the relationship between policymakers and academia was complicated by the difficulties for central bank practitionerswho largely followed a historical, more informal kind of reasoningto place systemic risk thinking in mathematical models, and for academic economists to engage in real-world systemic risk observations using their mathematical models (Thiemann, Aldegwy, et al., 2018).

In other words, the different incentives and objectives of research between these two groups promoted different methods and theoretical approaches to systemic risk. Practitioners working in central banks and International Organizations (IOs), like the BIS and the IMF, employed the more informal style of economic reasoning, which is more flexible in its methodological toolkit and more problem-driven in its analysis (Thiemann, Aldegwy, et al., 2018). In their view, systemic risk was also created endogenously, through common exposures and network effects, as well as building up over the financial cycle (Borio, 2003). However, these analyses were mostly narrative, lacking models and econometric techniques. Maximizing the policy effects of these pre-crisis informal macroprudential ideas for the post-crisis reform agenda would require their adoption in the formal language of economics (for macroprudential capital controls, Gallagher, 2016). The formal/informal divide in methods and styles of reasoning between these two, however, created frictions in the ideational transfer between these fields (Thiemann, Aldegwy, et al., 2018). The adoption of certain macroprudential standards post-crisis raises the questions of how interaction unfolded between the formal, predominantly academic field, which largely excluded endogenous systemic risk, and the informal field dominated by practitioners and economic historians, and how these processes influenced the policy measures adopted.

Such a translation of ideas from the non-formal to the formalized mode of economics might have been facilitated by the trend of increasing academic output by central bank researchers, called the 'scientization' of central banks (Marcussen, 2009), a process beginning in the 1990s that accelerated post-crisis (Dietsch et al., 2018;Jacobs & King, 2016). Through the establishment of research departments with independent funding, central bank economists have become academic actors themselves and legitimate producers of scientific knowledge (Dietsch et al., 2018, p. 86;Marcussen, 2009Marcussen, , 2013;;Mudge & Vauchez, 2016). Consequently, central banks are no longer solely dependent on academia but have become semi-autonomous agents with their own instruments, working conduct and interests (Marcussen, 2011). This newly amassed analytical capacity gained importance after the financial crisis, as central banks were tasked with implementing a macroprudential framework and had to co-produce analytical frameworks to that effect (Thiemann, Birk, et al., 2018). Often unsatisfied by the speed and pace of existing academic endeavors, central bankers would take some of these matters into their own hands, as we detail below. Our large-scale dataset enables these dynamics to be captured and the interactions between these different groups to be understood by looking at who writes about systemic risk, how the above frameworks are received in the field, and what their final impact is. We complement these findings with expert interviews to gain a more in-depth understanding of quantitative trends.

sections.paper_id: Thiemann et al. - 2021 - Measuring and mitigating systemic risks how the forging of new alliances between central bank and a
paragraphs: Analyzing newly formed alliances between academic economists and practical economists for measuring systemic risk requires exhaustive analysis of all available systemic risk concepts. More importantly, it requires locating successful and unsuccessful systemic risk measures to identify the characteristics of those that function as hinges between both fields. Here, an unsupervised text analysis method approaches the text corpus without prior theoretical assumptions about the expected sociological properties of systemic risk measures.

To obtain the most exhaustive collection of systemic risk measures, we use the RePEc (Research Papers in Economics) databasethe largest collection of economic publications, including academic publications and output of institutions such as central banks, BIS, IMF, think-tanks and financial institutions. From there, we retrieved every publication using the terms 'systemic risk' or 'macroprudential' 2 in either keyword, title, or abstract, yielding 5732 publications. Beyond the title, abstract and keywords, RePEc also provided relevant meta-data such as author affiliation for each publication. Elimination of duplicates left a dataset of 4212 entries in the period 1988-2017. Of these 4212, 2397 were available as PDFs, which we used for full-text analysis via Author-Topic-Modeling (ATM). 3 This method enables clear association of different authors to different topics that emerge from the texts (Rosen-Zvi et al., 2010). 4 This allows us to trace the professional affiliations of the authors that populate different topics, which is crucial for detecting the development of hinges. After downloading and preparing the dataset for textual analysis, we therefore coded every author in the dataset based on institutional affiliation as given in the document itself. The possible categories for authors are BIS, IMF, academics, central bankers, private finance, mixed, 5 and others (see Figure 1 and Table 1 for the distribution).

We split the sample into multiple cumulative periods to better understand how new concepts and systemic risk measures evolve and accumulate over time. 6 The first period, T1, covers 1988-2003 7 ; the second, T2, includes all publications from the beginning of the sample until the onset of the financial crisis in 2008. T3 includes T1 and T2 and extends to 2013, 8 with the entire sample, T4, to 2017. This led to the following distribution for documents and authors in each period, growing from 8 topics with 130 authors in T1 to 50 topics and 3496 authors in T4, expressing the growing interest in the topic from 2008 onwards (see Table 2) 9 : After analyzing all the topics related to systemic risk measurement for their appeal to both fields, we successively narrowed our analysis to topics that appeal to both. Utilizing document analysis and nine insider interviews (with authors at the Fed, ECB and academia), we then focused on a selection of specific systemic risk measures, their development, and how their specific constitution allows them to function as hinges between the two fields and enable effective policymaking.

Here, an unsupervised text analysis method approaches the text corpus without prior theoretical assumptions about the expected sociological properties of systemic risk measures.

To obtain the most exhaustive collection of systemic risk measures, we use the RePEc (Research Papers in Economics) databasethe largest collection of economic publications, including academic publications and output of institutions such as central banks, BIS, IMF, think-tanks and financial institutions. From there, we retrieved every publication using the terms 'systemic risk' or 'macroprudential' 2 in either keyword, title, or abstract, yielding 5732 publications. Beyond the title, abstract and keywords, RePEc also provided relevant meta-data such as author affiliation for each publication. Elimination of duplicates left a dataset of 4212 entries in the period 1988-2017. Of these 4212, 2397 were available as PDFs, which we used for full-text analysis via Author-Topic-Modeling (ATM). 3 This method enables clear association of different authors to different topics that emerge from the texts (Rosen-Zvi et al., 2010). 4 This allows us to trace the professional affiliations of the authors that populate different topics, which is crucial for detecting the development of hinges. After downloading and preparing the dataset for textual analysis, we therefore coded every author in the dataset based on institutional affiliation as given in the document itself. The possible categories for authors are BIS, IMF, academics, central bankers, private finance, mixed, 5 and others (see Figure 1 and Table 1 for the distribution).

We split the sample into multiple cumulative periods to better understand how new concepts and systemic risk measures evolve and accumulate over time. 6 The first period, T1, covers 1988-2003 7 ; the second, T2, includes all publications from the beginning of the sample until the onset of the financial crisis in 2008. T3 includes T1 and T2 and extends to 2013, 8 with the entire sample, T4, to 2017. This led to the following distribution for documents and authors in each period, growing from 8 topics with 130 authors in T1 to 50 topics and 3496 authors in T4, expressing the growing interest in the topic from 2008 onwards (see Table 2) 9 : After analyzing all the topics related to systemic risk measurement for their appeal to both fields, we successively narrowed our analysis to topics that appeal to both. Utilizing document analysis and nine insider interviews (with authors at the Fed, ECB and academia), we then focused on a selection of specific systemic risk measures, their development, and how their specific constitution allows them to function as hinges between the two fields and enable effective policymaking.

sections.header_raw: Analysis of risk modeling and measurement discourse
sections.header_norm: analysis of risk modeling and measurement discourse
paragraphs: Throughout the four subsamples, the following risk-related topics were identified: 'Risk Measurement and Modeling' in T1, which splits into 'Default Risk and Market-Based Risk Transfer' and 'Risk Management on a Macro Level' in T2. In T3, we identified 'Default Risk and Market-Based Risk Transfer', 'Tail Risk Modeling', 'Measuring Individual Systemic Risk Contribution' and 'Early Warning Systems/Composite Indicators of Financial Stress' as distinct topics. In T4, these topics largely remain stable, named 'Default Risk and Market-Based Risk Transfer', 'Tail Risk Modeling', 'Most Important Systemic Risk Measures: CoVar, EMS, SRISK' and 'Early Warning Systems' (see Figure 2 and Appendix A).

The topics can be distinguished across two scales. First, one can distinguish between a top-down, macroprudential approach to systemic risk and a bottom-up, microprudential approach (Borio, 2003). A bottom-up approach seeks to detect systemic risk where 'the overall fragility of the financial system reflects the summation of the individual fragilities of financial institutions' (FSB et al., 2009a, p. 17). Exemplary for this perspective is the assessment of risk transfer through the sale of specific financial instruments (Credit Default Swaps [CDS], Collateralized Debt Obligations [CDO]) or through institutions and actors. The aim is to combine idiosyncratic and systemic risk factors to assess individual risk exposure, risk-sharing between agents, or systemic risk contribution to the system (e.g. Krahnen & Wilde, 2006). The top-down approach, meanwhile, is 'examining the fragility of the overall financial system, focusing on the assessment of the (often complex) interactions of the components' (FSB et al., 2009a, p. 18), using aggregate data (credit growth and asset prices) and seeks to develop aggregate financial stress indices (e.g. Oet et al., 2012). Relatedly, topics can be distinguished according to their author populations, across the scale of central bank economists through to pure academics.

These scales jointly clarify the persistence of different reasoning in different parts of economics as an intellectual field (Whitley, 2000). Academics on average apply a much more bottom-up, market-oriented and micro-founded approach, whereas practitionersand most importantly, central bankerson average use a top-down and macroprudential approach to the market. The analysis further reveals that the formal/informal research divide identified by Thiemann, Aldegwy, et al. ( 2018), as well as Whitley's divide into scientific and intellectual fields of research (2000), largely persists throughout the risk measurement and modeling discourse. However, it also shows that the affiliations of authors (academics or practitioners) that populate topics can shift over time when these topics allow economists to obtain the type of merit that is rewarded within their fields. Moreover, within these topics, important alliances between practitioners and academics can form, as we detail below. To illustrate these shifting author affiliations to topics over time, Figure 3 shows this topic development graphically by incorporating findings relating to the kind of economists researching these topics. The following analysis chronologically follows the evolution of the topics regarding their assigned author population, their meaning and focus of the topic papers.

In the first period, there is only one topic, 'Risk Measurement and Modeling' (T1). It is an almost purely academic topic and with no clear content focus beyond the measurement of different kinds of risks and market developments. These range from the measurement of stock market volatility through margin requirements; stochastic and volatility models to pricing models. Notably, however, it already includes a critical discussion of Value at Risk models, which are based on market data that are seen as endogenous to the system and subject to pro-cyclical amplifications and hence unreliable (Danielsson, 2000). The small size and broad content scale of the topic reflects the infancy of the research on systemic risk measurement at the time. In 2003, the start of sample period T2, Claudio Borio published his seminal paper on systemic risk and macroprudential regulation, laying the foundations of the macroprudential approach to regulation and, initially for practitioners, in particular, a new field of research. Consequently, the onset of a trend toward a formal/informal divide in the topic content and assigned author populations can be observed. The more 'formal' topic, 'Default Risk and Market-based Risk Transfer', that emerges in T2 and persists until T4 is still a predominantly academic topic (67%). It is more micro-foundational and focuses on improving existing risk measures. Of all the topics, its content is the least uniform as it comprises systemic risk measurements but also work on counterparty risk and risk transfer through structured products and the systemic risks it might generate (risk transfer through CDOs and CDSs: Krahnen & Wilde, 2006;Ueno & Baba, 2006). It has a microeconomic focus in that it also deals with improving risk models employed by financial institutions (Malone et al., 2009), models to compute the risk of single financial instruments (CoCo Bonds: Brigo et al., 2015), and models to assess the effect of systemic risk on a single institution (Suh et al., 2013). Interconnectedness and default correlation through portfolio simulations are also central to the topic, which illustrates the bottom-up view on systemic risk. The author population is predominantly academic, illustrating academic researchers' more micro-oriented and market-based view of risk (see Figure 2).

The second topic in T2, 'Risk Management on a Macro Level', is more heterogeneous in author population, with 53% academics, a substantial proportion of central bankers (25%), and economists in international organizations (9%). This is also reflected in increasing policy-focused research. There is increased research on measurements of banking sector soundness and fragility for bank supervisors (Krainer & Lopez, 2008;Goodhart et al., 2006). Nevertheless, the divide is still weak but becomes clearer in the next period. After 2008, in T3, a further topic splits off from 'Default Risk Modeling', namely the highly econometric and academic 'Tail Risk Modeling', which persists until T4. It is the core topic of the formal side of the divide and has the highest proportion of academics (78% in T3, 87% in T4). With 63 affiliated authors in T3 and 62 in T4, it is a very robust topic. It had particular momentum after the financial crisis materialized in 2008, which was considered a tail risk event. The central topics are tail dependence measurements (e.g. van Oordt & Zhou, 2012) and alternative systemic risk measurements based on Extreme Value Theory (e.g. Glasserman et al., 2015;Wang et al., 2013).

The papers strongly rely on econometrics and, already in T3, there is a remarkable difference in their policy orientation. The few central bank and IO-affiliated papers check proposed systemic risk measurements for their 'reliability and robustness' in terms of tail risk analysis for financial regulation (L€ offler & Raupach, 2013, p. 1;Rocco, 2014). The academic papers, in turn, often start without reference to the financial crisis or to financial regulation. In their extreme, they are solely mathematical papers published by engineers or physicists under the subject of econophysics 10 (for purely mathematical papers in the sample, see e.g. Bernard & Czado, 2015). Through its predominant academic author population, the heavy emphasis on econometrics and the weak reference to policy or real-world application, 'Tail Risk Modeling' further exemplifies the divide between academics and applied economists.

Diametrically opposed in terms of author affiliation is 'Early Warning and Composite Indicators of Financial Distress', which first emerges in T3 and is the archetypical topic for the policy-oriented side of the divide. This topic is predominantly populated by applied economists from CBs and the IMF both in T3 and in T4. In T3, pure practitioners (CB, IMF, BIS) make up 60% of affiliated authors, not counting mixed affiliation (10%). In T4, pure practitioners make up 67% of the affiliated authors and mixed practitioners 12% (see Figure 2), indicating growing interest from the policy side. 11 Aiming at developing Early Warning Systems (EWS) for supervisory and policymaking bodies, many authors propose EWS that produce a binary index of 0 or 1 to indicate the probability of crisis occurrence (Papadopoulos et al., 2016). EWS are intended to detect financial distress early and predict systemic events (see, e.g. Zigraiova & Jakub ık, 2014). The topic has high policy relevance and fully incorporates macroprudential thinking, employing a topdown perspective to the market. In this vein, most of the publications are published by central banks like the Macroprudential Research Network (MaRS) of the ECB and international organizations such as the IMF (Alessi et al., 2015;Arsov et al., 2013;Blancher et al., 2013).

The topic 'Measuring Individual Systemic Risk Contribution' also emerges in T3. The author population is heterogeneous, numbering approximately 50% academics, 41% applied economists and 10% from private finance. Also for this topic, events in 2008 gave momentum to the elaboration of new systemic risk models and identification of Systemically Important Financial Institutions (SIFIs), which was especially important for supervisory authorities seeking to designate SIFIs (see e.g. Agarwal et al., 2013;Price & Walter, 2011). The data used in the papers to compute systemic risk and importance is diverse and ranges from CDS data (see e.g. Suh et al., 2013) to stock return information (Gravelle & Li, 2013). Based on content analysis of the papers in this topic, it becomes evident that the three most important measurements in the discussion about individual systemic risk contribution measures are Marginal Expected Shortfall (MES), CoVar and SRISK. All are based on market data, as most papers within the topic discuss, extend and apply these measures (see, e.g. Agarwal et al., 2013;Benoit et al., 2013).

Whereas both MES and SRISK propose measuring the capital shortfall of an individual bank conditional on the banking system as a whole being in distress, CoVar captures the contribution of an institution in distress to the distress of the entire financial system. In this sense, MES/SRISK and CoVar are specular measures. The first two, using the technique of expected shortfall, indicate banks that are likely to suffer most, given system-wide distress, and are of interest to supervisors and investors seeking to locate frail institutions in times of crisis. CoVaR, meanwhile, extends the traditional Value at Risk Model by focusing on tail risks and singles out the banks whose distress is most likely to contribute to systemwide distress, allowing for anticipatory reinforcement of these banks by regulators. To identify the contribution to system-wide stress by individual banks, CoVar investigates the co-movement of Value at Risk measures of a bank and the financial system as a whole. It focuses on the contribution of a bank entering into more troubled territory in terms of its Value at Risk (entering into the tail of the distribution) to the system as a whole (a measure called delta CoVaR). It uses quantile regressions, a technique to model tail risks that allows it to capture this tail co-variance of individual banks and the system.

Central bank economists were often skeptical of these market data-driven approaches favored by academic economists and instead emphasized the supervisory perspective using balance sheet data (see e.g. de Bandt et al., 2013;Tavolaro & Visnovsky, 2014). Their efforts highlight the importance of practicality and applicability of risk measurement within policy and regulation. Given the post-crisis insistence that systemic risk is more than the sum of idiosyncratic risk, coherence between a metric for individual systemic risk capable of adding up coherently to an overall systemic risk measure is a major concern within central bank and BIS/ IMF papers in that period, as expressed, for example, in the paper by Tarashev, Borio and Tsatsaronis from the BIS (2010). In it, they propose a methodology to attribute systemic risk to financial institutions from one of the composite measures of systemic risk (p. 3), setting out a top-down approach to measuring systemic risk which decomposes a single systemic risk metric into individual systemic risk contributions. They criticize using MES, SRISK and CoVar for regulatory purposes, pointing to how the former two measure the impact of 'participation of banks in systemic events', whereas regulators need measures for banks' contribution to these systemic events (p. 2). CoVar on the other hand, while doing just that, is criticized for not coherently adding up to an overall measure of systemic risk (Tarashev et al., 2020).

After 2013 (in T4), the topic further crystallizes around these three most important risk measurements. So much so, that now their names are prominent on the wordlist for the topic itself (see Appendix A). 12 This topic, which we named 'Most important SR Measures: CoVaR, MES and SRISK', is now mostly populated by pure academics (69%) and highly quantitative in methods (see Figure 2). Content wise, the publications contain extensions, comparisons and applications of these three measures (see e.g. Banulescu & Dumitrescu, 2015;Mainik & Schaanning, 2014). All of the practitioners associated with the predecessor topic 'Measuring Individual Systemic Risk Contribution' in T3, including Tarashev et al. (2010), fall below the association threshold in the 'Most important SR Measures: CoVaR, MES and SRISK', indicating the growing relevance of academic research in the field. Between T3 and T4more specifically between 2010 and 2017the topic transforms from being a predominantly policy-relevant and rather practically oriented topic to one that is academic and dominated by marginal quantitative advancements.

The developments after 2010 indicate that as G-SIFI measuresleaning on CoVaR for legitimacybecame operational, academics became more involved in the issue and research on these measurements brought them scientific rewards. The most important indicator for this change is that the three papers containing the original contributions on the most prominent risk measures, after a long review process, were published in highly ranked academic journals between 2016 and 2017. 13 Hence, the measures developed by academics and central bankers in the aftermath of the crisis start to affect the scientific field and acquire the reputation of pure science worthy of scientific merit through publication and citations, in a notable reversal of the usual model of regulatory science. In short, the findings on author populations within the relevant topics and publication processes reveal an evolution from, at first, solely policy-relevant work toward a scientific topic with which academics engage and receive rewards within their field. This shift demonstrates that the measures developed in the aftermath of the crisis act as a hinge between academics and central bankers.

In line with this hinge characteristic, the topic 'Measuring Individual Systemic Risk Contribution' is the most mixed of all topics in terms of author affiliation. It thereby contrasts with the divide between applied and academic economists we detected for the topic 'Early Warning Systems', the archetype of the informal and policy-relevant research field, and for 'Tail Risk Modeling', the archetype for the formal and less policy-focused research field. 'Measuring Individual Systemic Risk Contribution' thus marks the overlap between pure academic and pure practitioner topics. By informing the search for proper risk measurements of systemically important institutions for policymakers and engaging in the use of advanced econometric techniques to model tail risks such as quantile regressions, this topic combines academic and practitioners' interests. It therefore appears between the two archetype topics in Figure 3. Figure 4 illustrates the overlap of systemic risk discourses between practitioners and academics and locates different risk measurement models according to relevance within the respective discourse. This is not to say that there is complete agreement in the topic of 'Measuring Individual Systemic Risk Contribution'. As sustained efforts to conceptualize systemic risk are a relatively recent phenomenon, there are competing and, to some extent, even contradicting approaches to its measurement within this topic (Bisias et al., 2012, p. 1). This diversity stems from the lack of consensus on a clear definition and, most importantly, from the difference in the micro-or macroprudential conception of systemic risk used by academics and applied economists respectively. On a methodological level, the divergence in micro-and macroprudential approaches is expressed in the data employed, whereby the measures of systemic risk contributions of individual banks rely either on balance sheet or market data (de Bandt et al., 2013).

While balance sheet data employed in the microprudential approach suffers from its retrospective nature, problems of comparability and availability, the conceptual problem for macroprudential experts vis-a-vis market data is much larger because it poses a paradox. Market data build on market prices and hence reproduces market perceptions, rather than the value of fundamentals (De Bandt et al., 2013, p. 18). From the start, however, macroprudential thinking implied that risk is created endogenously and that markets misprice risks (Persaud, 2001). Consequently, regulators can hardly use market data to correct these markets' tendency toward boom and bust (Crockett, 2000). Most importantly, the fact that the pricing of risk shortly before a crisis is often much reduced whereas actual risks continue to grow (what is called 'the volatility paradox'), speaks against such uses (see Borio, 2009;Brunnermeier et al., 2009). This stance effectively ruled out simple measurements based on market data when regulators wanted to adopt measurements in line with macroprudential thinking. However, developing systemic risk measurements based on market data is exactly what academics, who tried to reconcile systemic risk models with the macroprudential line of post-crisis thinking, have been doing. How, if at all, has this contradiction between academics and practitioners been resolved?

Given the post-crisis insistence that systemic risk is more than the sum of idiosyncratic risk, coherence between a metric for individual systemic risk capable of adding up coherently to an overall systemic risk measure is a major concern within central bank and BIS/ IMF papers in that period, as expressed, for example, in the paper by Tarashev, Borio and Tsatsaronis from the BIS (2010). In it, they propose a methodology to attribute systemic risk to financial institutions from one of the composite measures of systemic risk (p. 3), setting out a top-down approach to measuring systemic risk which decomposes a single systemic risk metric into individual systemic risk contributions. They criticize using MES, SRISK and CoVar for regulatory purposes, pointing to how the former two measure the impact of 'participation of banks in systemic events', whereas regulators need measures for banks' contribution to these systemic events (p. 2). CoVar on the other hand, while doing just that, is criticized for not coherently adding up to an overall measure of systemic risk (Tarashev et al., 2020).

After 2013 (in T4), the topic further crystallizes around these three most important risk measurements. So much so, that now their names are prominent on the wordlist for the topic itself (see Appendix A). 12 This topic, which we named 'Most important SR Measures: CoVaR, MES and SRISK', is now mostly populated by pure academics (69%) and highly quantitative in methods (see Figure 2). Content wise, the publications contain extensions, comparisons and applications of these three measures (see e.g. Banulescu & Dumitrescu, 2015;Mainik & Schaanning, 2014). All of the practitioners associated with the predecessor topic 'Measuring Individual Systemic Risk Contribution' in T3, including Tarashev et al. (2010), fall below the association threshold in the 'Most important SR Measures: CoVaR, MES and SRISK', indicating the growing relevance of academic research in the field. Between T3 and T4more specifically between 2010 and 2017the topic transforms from being a predominantly policy-relevant and rather practically oriented topic to one that is academic and dominated by marginal quantitative advancements.

The developments after 2010 indicate that as G-SIFI measuresleaning on CoVaR for legitimacybecame operational, academics became more involved in the issue and research on these measurements brought them scientific rewards. The most important indicator for this change is that the three papers containing the original contributions on the most prominent risk measures, after a long review process, were published in highly ranked academic journals between 2016 and 2017. 13 Hence, the measures developed by academics and central bankers in the aftermath of the crisis start to affect the scientific field and acquire the reputation of pure science worthy of scientific merit through publication and citations, in a notable reversal of the usual model of regulatory science. In short, the findings on author populations within the relevant topics and publication processes reveal an evolution from, at first, solely policy-relevant work toward a scientific topic with which academics engage and receive rewards within their field. This shift demonstrates that the measures developed in the aftermath of the crisis act as a hinge between academics and central bankers.

In line with this hinge characteristic, the topic 'Measuring Individual Systemic Risk Contribution' is the most mixed of all topics in terms of author affiliation. It thereby contrasts with the divide between applied and academic economists we detected for the topic 'Early Warning Systems', the archetype of the informal and policy-relevant research field, and for 'Tail Risk Modeling', the archetype for the formal and less policy-focused research field. By informing the search for proper risk measurements of systemically important institutions for policymakers and engaging in the use of advanced econometric techniques to model tail risks such as quantile regressions, this topic combines academic and practitioners' interests. It therefore appears between the two archetype topics in Figure 3. Figure 4 illustrates the overlap of systemic risk discourses between practitioners and academics and locates different risk measurement models according to relevance within the respective discourse. This is not to say that there is complete agreement in the topic of 'Measuring Individual Systemic Risk Contribution'. As sustained efforts to conceptualize systemic risk are a relatively recent phenomenon, there are competing and, to some extent, even contradicting approaches to its measurement within this topic (Bisias et al., 2012, p. 1). This diversity stems from the lack of consensus on a clear definition and, most importantly, from the difference in the micro-or macroprudential conception of systemic risk used by academics and applied economists respectively. On a methodological level, the divergence in micro-and macroprudential approaches is expressed in the data employed, whereby the measures of systemic risk contributions of individual banks rely either on balance sheet or market data (de Bandt et al., 2013).

While balance sheet data employed in the microprudential approach suffers from its retrospective nature, problems of comparability and availability, the conceptual problem for macroprudential experts vis-a-vis market data is much larger because it poses a paradox. Market data build on market prices and hence reproduces market perceptions, rather than the value of fundamentals (De Bandt et al., 2013, p. 18). From the start, however, macroprudential thinking implied that risk is created endogenously and that markets misprice risks (Persaud, 2001). Consequently, regulators can hardly use market data to correct these markets' tendency toward boom and bust (Crockett, 2000). Most importantly, the fact that the pricing of risk shortly before a crisis is often much reduced whereas actual risks continue to grow (what is called 'the volatility paradox'), speaks against such uses (see Borio, 2009;Brunnermeier et al., 2009). This stance effectively ruled out simple measurements based on market data when regulators wanted to adopt measurements in line with macroprudential thinking. However, developing systemic risk measurements based on market data is exactly what academics, who tried to reconcile systemic risk models with the macroprudential line of post-crisis thinking, have been doing. How, if at all, has this contradiction between academics and practitioners been resolved?

Throughout the four subsamples, the following risk-related topics were identified: 'Risk Measurement and Modeling' in T1, which splits into 'Default Risk and Market-Based Risk Transfer' and 'Risk Management on a Macro Level' in T2. In T3, we identified 'Default Risk and Market-Based Risk Transfer', 'Tail Risk Modeling', 'Measuring Individual Systemic Risk Contribution' and 'Early Warning Systems/Composite Indicators of Financial Stress' as distinct topics. In T4, these topics largely remain stable, named 'Default Risk and Market-Based Risk Transfer', 'Tail Risk Modeling', 'Most Important Systemic Risk Measures: CoVar, EMS, SRISK' and 'Early Warning Systems' (see Figure 2 and Appendix A).

The topics can be distinguished across two scales. First, one can distinguish between a top-down, macroprudential approach to systemic risk and a bottom-up, microprudential approach (Borio, 2003). A bottom-up approach seeks to detect systemic risk where 'the overall fragility of the financial system reflects the summation of the individual fragilities of financial institutions' (FSB et al., 2009a, p. 17). Exemplary for this perspective is the assessment of risk transfer through the sale of specific financial instruments (Credit Default Swaps [CDS], Collateralized Debt Obligations [CDO]) or through institutions and actors. The aim is to combine idiosyncratic and systemic risk factors to assess individual risk exposure, risk-sharing between agents, or systemic risk contribution to the system (e.g. Krahnen & Wilde, 2006). The top-down approach, meanwhile, is 'examining the fragility of the overall financial system, focusing on the assessment of the (often complex) interactions of the components' (FSB et al., 2009a, p. 18), using aggregate data (credit growth and asset prices) and seeks to develop aggregate financial stress indices (e.g. Oet et al., 2012). Relatedly, topics can be distinguished according to their author populations, across the scale of central bank economists through to pure academics.

These scales jointly clarify the persistence of different reasoning in different parts of economics as an intellectual field (Whitley, 2000). Academics on average apply a much more bottom-up, market-oriented and micro-founded approach, whereas practitionersand most importantly, central bankerson average use a top-down and macroprudential approach to the market. The analysis further reveals that the formal/informal research divide identified by Thiemann, Aldegwy, et al. ( 2018), as well as Whitley's divide into scientific and intellectual fields of research (2000), largely persists throughout the risk measurement and modeling discourse. However, it also shows that the affiliations of authors (academics or practitioners) that populate topics can shift over time when these topics allow economists to obtain the type of merit that is rewarded within their fields. Moreover, within these topics, important alliances between practitioners and academics can form, as we detail below. To illustrate these shifting author affiliations to topics over time, Figure 3 shows this topic development graphically by incorporating findings relating to the kind of economists researching these topics. The following analysis chronologically follows the evolution of the topics regarding their assigned author population, their meaning and focus of the topic papers.

In the first period, there is only one topic, 'Risk Measurement and Modeling' (T1). It is an almost purely academic topic and with no clear content focus beyond the measurement of different kinds of risks and market developments. These range from the measurement of stock market volatility through margin requirements; stochastic and volatility models to pricing models. Notably, however, it already includes a critical discussion of Value at Risk models, which are based on market data that are seen as endogenous to the system and subject to pro-cyclical amplifications and hence unreliable (Danielsson, 2000). The small size and broad content scale of the topic reflects the infancy of the research on systemic risk measurement at the time. In 2003, the start of sample period T2, Claudio Borio published his seminal paper on systemic risk and macroprudential regulation, laying the foundations of the macroprudential approach to regulation and, initially for practitioners, in particular, a new field of research. Consequently, the onset of a trend toward a formal/informal divide in the topic content and assigned author populations can be observed. The more 'formal' topic, 'Default Risk and Market-based Risk Transfer', that emerges in T2 and persists until T4 is still a predominantly academic topic (67%). It is more micro-foundational and focuses on improving existing risk measures. Of all the topics, its content is the least uniform as it comprises systemic risk measurements but also work on counterparty risk and risk transfer through structured products and the systemic risks it might generate (risk transfer through CDOs and CDSs: Krahnen & Wilde, 2006;Ueno & Baba, 2006). It has a microeconomic focus in that it also deals with improving risk models employed by financial institutions (Malone et al., 2009), models to compute the risk of single financial instruments (CoCo Bonds: Brigo et al., 2015), and models to assess the effect of systemic risk on a single institution (Suh et al., 2013). Interconnectedness and default correlation through portfolio simulations are also central to the topic, which illustrates the bottom-up view on systemic risk. The author population is predominantly academic, illustrating academic researchers' more micro-oriented and market-based view of risk (see Figure 2).

This is also reflected in increasing policy-focused research. There is increased research on measurements of banking sector soundness and fragility for bank supervisors (Krainer & Lopez, 2008;Goodhart et al., 2006). sections.paper_id: Thiemann et al. - 2021 - Measuring and mitigating systemic risks how the forging of new alliances between central bank and a
sections.header_raw: Interaction between academic and applied approaches and their translation into CoVaR, MES and SRISK
sections.header_norm: interaction between academic and applied approaches and their translation into covar mes and srisk
paragraphs: The most impactful systemic risk measures in our sample in terms of resonance in the academic field 14 and among regulators were the three individual risk measures: MES (Acharya et al., 2010), 15 Conditional Value at Risk (CoVar) (Adrian & Brunnermeier, 2008, 2016) and the SRISK measure (Brownlees & Engle, 2017). These were developed during and right after the crisis by six academics at NYU Stern School of Business or by a central bank economist at the Fed New York collaborating with a prestigious Princeton academic. Two of these three measures were published as working papers at Federal Reserve Banks and all three of them rely on market data. And yet, despite their common popularity in the overall discussion and their explicit engagement with regulatory issues, CoVaR received more recognition by the regulatory community. This is partly due to the different foci of these measures explained above. As regulators were seeking to designate systemically important financial institutions whose failure might provoke systemic turmoil, the focus from distressed banks to the whole system was more appealing (Tarashev et al., 2010). However, a content analysis of the three papers reveals further reasons why CoVar has been more appealing to practitioners, who have positively referenced it when reporting back to the G20 on how they intend to build their G-SIFI measure (FSB et al., 2009b, p. 30f). This is becauseadjusting for the way practitioners use market data and adopting a longitudinal approachthe measure displays much more sensitivity to macroprudential concerns with respect to the reliability of market data.

This sensitivity can be seen in the length of the data series used to construct the measure, 16 but also with respect to how that market data are used. First, CoVar manipulates the data to generate not only a cross-sectional but also a forward-looking measure of systemic risk and pending crises (called Forward-CoVar). This seeks to provide an EWS for regulators to intervene during the build-up of systemic risk rather than only during its unfolding. This approach explicitly allows the authors to account for the volatility paradox deemed central by the macroprudential community (Adrian & Brunnermeier, 2008, p. 19). Second, while all three measures link their findings regarding the systemic risk contribution of banks to the balance sheet size and leverage of the financial institutions, Adrian and Brunnermeier draw a different conclusion. Their study shows these variables to be highly correlated with CoVaR, so for future regulatory action, they suggest substituting CoVaR with measures for balance sheet size, leverage and the structure of the asset and liability side. In this way, they legitimate the balance sheet-based SIFI measure finally adopted through measurements based on market data.

This sensitivity to the preferred approach of practitioners arguably stems from the professional positioning of its authors who seek to allow the regulatory community to move beyond market data with the help of market data. In contrast, the other two measures and their authors, while holding high epistemic authority in the academic field, do not acknowledge to the same extent macroprudential concerns in the community of applied central banks and instead mostly insist on the efficiency of easily replicable systemic risk measurements based on publicly available market data (see also Benoit et al., 2013). Hence, while Engle received a Nobel Prize in 2002 for his work on time series analysis and risk measurement, and Acharya is the top economist in our entire sample according to the RePEc measurement, they cannot translate their prominence into regulatory impact. They simply lack the sensitivity of the authors of CoVar to the concerns posited by the macroprudential epistemic community, which further contributes to the measures' appeal to the regulatory community.

This sensitivity was generated through the daily interactions that one of the authors, a central bank economist, had with these debates in the Fed at the same time that the other author was engaged with the macroprudential debates on the academic side when authoring the Geneva report (Brunnermeier et al., 2009). 17 The authors themselves link responsiveness to these concerns to frequent interactions with Fed staff, which made them more aware of these sensitivities (academic author interview, January 25, 2018). Through this sensitivity, CoVaR, a central systemic risk measure in the economic discourse post-crisis, becomes one of the epistemic foundations to justify the new policy device of systemic risk surcharges for global systemically important financial institutions (G-SIFI). At the same time, the technical maturity of CoVar, being based on quantile regressions, made it very appealing to the academic community (academic economist interview, January 6, 2019), a fact which can be directly linked to the scientization of central banks who increasingly employ top-notch economists. Exemplarily, Tobias Adrian, the coauthor of CoVar, holds an economics PhD from MIT.
Discussion and conclusion: the professional origins of systemic risk measures and their usability for systemic risk monitoring

The modified conception of systemic risk constitutes the centerpiece of the shift from micro-to macroprudential thinking post-crisis and is crucial for the finally implemented macroprudential regulation. In investigating economic debates on how to measure systemic risks, we showed that the applied versus academic economics divide structured the debate. While there is continued divergence on the techniques, assumptions and outcomes of research by central bank economists and academics, we also identify areas of increased collaboration, such as for the most prominent post-crisis systemic risk measures. Despite the general trend of persistent bifurcation in our sample, we find a trend of intensifying debate between central bank economists as well as academics on the topic of policy-relevant systemic risk measurements, establishing the issue as an object of interest for both fields. This deviation from the general trend stems from the fact that risk models unify theory and practice and are therefore a concern to economists in both the intellectual and the scientific fields. This is evidenced in our results, as both groups engage with risk modeling and measurement techniques, albeit within their own task-specific reward systems.

This increasing collaboration brings together work on tail risks and EWS. The most interesting point of intersection in this respect concerns the individual systemic risk measures developed since the crisis, which engages both academics and central bank practitioners. Most notable is the rising prominence of these measures in the academic economic discourse, itself provoking important uptake by both practitioners and academics. The discourse on systemic risk measurement and modeling is therefore a case in which technocratic economists and academic financial economists enter a new post-crisis alliance, whereby certain topics, driven by practical and academic interests, generate collaborations between academics and central bankers. These collaborations act as a hinge, producing rewards both in the academic field and in the field of central banking. In this respect, the analysis points toward an increasing openness of central bankers toward cooperation with financial economists, a cooperation which, until the crisis, had been confined primarily to monetary economists (academic interview, December 7, 2018). In advancing this new field, the scientization of central banks plays a central role due to the work by applied economists in these institutions having become of the highest academic quality.

Given their engagement in actual policy work, however, their papers also seek to provide a theoretical, rational and new outlook on the world which legitimizes macroprudential interventions by central bank practitioners. Individual systemic risk measures such as CoVaR thereby represent an important step forward in legitimizing macroprudential interventions. More concrete than the pre-crisis works of Borio and other macroprudential thinkers, CoVaR's appeal stems not only from the central positioning of its proponents in the regulatory decision-making circles (Baker, 2013a), but also from its analytical content and the academic rigor with which it has been developed. To achieve such rigor, it repurposed techniques from financial economicsdeveloped to evaluate the riskiness of a portfolioto investigate the co-variance of banks' tail risks and the whole financial system. CoVaR is thereby incrementally altering techniques developed for one purpose to serve another. By using market data and modifying Value at Risk models, it provides the necessary continuity with the pre-crisis academic and regulatory discourse while also constituting an ontological break with the past, which was necessary to gain the endorsement of central bank practitioners. It paradoxically justifies an ontological doubt among this group that is inherent in the early macroprudential critique (markets cannot be trusted to self-regulate, see Baker, 2018;Danielsson, 2000;Persaud, 2001) with an epistemological approach based on market data.

Indeed, this paradox of using market data to contradict the political authority of markets was a powerful way to resolve the legitimation problem that financial regulation faced post-crisis, with limited evidence based on other techniques. This finding exemplifies the fragmented nature of the macroprudential shift (Kaya & Reay, 2019), where a radical shift in outlook on financial markets is coupled with incremental but meaningful change in policy devices. Here, change does not happen as a revolution but as a gradual process in which the epistemology slowly adjusts to a shift in ontology. Such an understanding contributes to a better grasp of the incremental steps taken with respect to macroprudential frameworks by central banks immediately after the crisis (Johnson et al., 2019;Kranke & Yarrow, 2019), while maintaining the possibility for longer term, more fundamental change based on this new hinge between the central banking community and the academic profession. Further work by these collaborating academics and central bankers then sought to fine-tune these findings and justify more wide-ranging interventions in what was perceived as dangerous build-ups of systemic risks. These works exhibit a certain path-dependency, in that they develop the themes of EWS jointly with tail risk analyses to provide rigorous model-based justifications for anti-cyclical macroprudential interventions.

The academic work of these scholars within and outside but always connected to central banks has therefore been an important source for the advancement of the macroprudential agenda in central bank decision-making. Operating at the boundary between regulation and science, these scholars seek to generate 'actionable knowledge' (Hirschman & Popp-Berman, 2014), metric systems which justify intervention. This new alliance means that central bankers today are no longer so alone when having to act on financial stability (Mabbett & Schelkle, 2019). They can rely on rigorous research that lives up to academic standards. Our case shows that beyond the strategic selection of economic theories in technocratic organizations to justify new policies, as described by Clift (2018Clift ( , 2019) ) and Ban (2015), there is a possibility for technocratic and academic economists to join forces and jointly produce new policy devices that can justify a new policy agenda. This finding extends beyond central banks and potentially to all institutions that employ economists who engage in research, such as international organizations (IMF, OECD) or ministries of finance.
